figures.caption: Figure 3: The three systems of study, their configurations, and their impacts.
Figure 4: A visual guide of the losses.
Figure 8: A 9-step rollout with true hardware, encoded-decoded, and latent ROM trajectories.
Figure 6: Decoded visualization of the boundary of Ωz in full-order coordinates of the G1. Plotted states correspond to the center-of-mass position relative to the stance foot.

Table 1: Summary of the loss terms. W denotes trainable weights (excluding biases), and g (k−1) ρ denotes repeated application of gρ. In addition, B denotes the mini-batch size and K denotes the trajectory length.

Figure 5: Per-step normalized error of the learned ROM over 3,000 test trajectories for each system. (Top) shows single-step reconstruction error, and (bottom) shows multi-step latent forward-propagation error. Solid curves denote the mean and shaded bands denote ±σ.

Table 6: Autoencoder ROM training details. The G1 state vector is 59-dimensional due to the quaternion representation of base orientation (nq = 30, nv = 29), though the underlying state space is 58-dimensional.

Figure 2: An attractive invariant manifold for a discrete-time system (in red) can have a lower dimensional embedding (in blue). This embedding informs a choice of latent dimension and reduced-order model.
